last_result.effect: EasyCrypt accepted a proof-state change; the refreshed view is based on the new committed state.
last_result.proof_state: The committed EasyCrypt proof state changed.

lines: &1 (left ) : {i : int, c2 : byte list, n, n0 : nonce,
x, x0, x1 : nonce * C.counter, c1 : bytes, r : poly_in,
t, t0, y : poly_out, z : block, a : associated_data,
p1, p2 : message, nap : nonce * associated_data * message,
p, p0 : plaintext, c, c0 : ciphertext, lt : tag list}
&2 (right) : {i : int, c2 : byte list, n, n0 : nonce,
x, x0, x1 : nonce * C.counter, c1 : bytes, r : poly_in,
t, t0, y, t1 : poly_out, z : block, a : associated_data,
p1, p2 : message, nap : nonce * associated_data * message,
ti : tag, p, p0 : plaintext, c, c0 : ciphertext, lt : tag list}
size UFCMA_l.lbad1{2} <= UFCMA_li.i{2} < size UFCMA_l.lbad1{2} + size lt{2}
let lbad1_R = UFCMA_l.lbad1{2} ++ map (fun (t' : tag) => (t1{2}, t')) lt{2}
UFCMA_l.lbad1{1} ++ map (fun (t' : tag) => (t0{1}, t')) lt{1} = lbad1_R /\
(nth (w1, w2) lbad1_R nth0).`1 = (nth (w1, w2) lbad1_R nth0).`2)

source.label: Parsed from EasyCrypt pretty goal text

navigation.confidence_reason: The current pRHL goal exposes a bad-event/instrumented branch together with sampling or one-sided suffix evidence; this is the shape where experts use `case`, `rcondt/rcondf`, `swap`, indexed `wp`, `conseq`, or one-sided `rnd` before local SMT. The fast-track tactic is schematic, so confidence is capped until the current view or an inspect result supplies concrete terms.
navigation.why_now: current pRHL state looks past the main opener: the useful next work is local branch/suffix surgery, not changing proof family.

preconditions: Use only when the visible goal has a concrete guard controlling the local branch.
After the case split, use `rcondt`/`rcondf` for forced branches, `swap` for statement order, indexed `wp` for suffixes, `conseq` for a smaller post, or `rnd{1}`/`rnd{2}` for one-sided samples.
If the guard fact needed for `rcondt`/`rcondf` is not in the precondition, treat that as an invariant repair signal.
Probe the smallest local block; do not fresh-restart just because this surgery is syntax-sensitive.
fast_track_probe.expected_next_shape: Branch-specific pRHL residuals where forced-condition, swap, indexed-wp, one-sided-random, or conseq steps can be checked.

anti_routes.reason: This is a branch-local/suffix alignment problem; a fresh restart throws away useful opener and invariant work.
One side has sampling or instrumentation before the suffix; use `rcond`, `swap`, or one-sided `rnd` before plain `wp`.
`sim` is usually too late/too strong before the guard and suffix shape are aligned.

repair_if_fails.next: inspect tactic_forms for swap, rcondt, rcondf, wp, conseq, rnd, or eager
inspect align or goal_info for statement indexes before retrying
use undo_to_checkpoint {} and choose the checkpoint before the call/loop invariant that should carry the missing guard or size fact
inspect lemma_hints or lookup_symbol for the local size/nth/bad-event lemma
downgrade this surgery route only after a smaller prefix and relevant tactic_forms fail

navigation.confidence_reason: The current view exposes pRHL/procedure frontier evidence, but the first instructions are not a directly aligned branch/call.
navigation.why_now: current pRHL/procedure frontier has both_sides_at_sample evidence; choose a structural step before local closure.

preconditions: Use only on the current pRHL/procedure frontier.
If the first instructions are not aligned, consume or align setup before branch tactics.
When one side has setup before an aligned branch, prefer indexed `sp i j` from the alignment counts.
Probe first; commit only the accepted structural step or prefix.
fast_track_probe.expected_next_shape: A smaller aligned frontier or clearer first-instruction shape.

anti_routes.reason: first instructions are left `sample` and right `sample`, so branch frontiers are not aligned.
Plain `sim.` needs EasyCrypt to infer the equality relation. At a pRHL frontier with visible call/setup structure, first make the relation explicit with call, seq/wp, alignment, or conseq.
anti_routes.usable_when: `sim.` becomes a good short tactic when the frontiers are already synchronized or after `conseq`/call/structural steps expose an explicit equality relation for the remaining instructions.

anti_routes.reason: Procedure/frontier goals usually need structural exposure before pure SMT.

repair_if_fails.next: inspect goal_info or align for first-instruction details
inspect call_subgoals/subgoal_gap; if generated obligations lack facts that should have been preserved, use undo_to_checkpoint {} before the call or loop invariant
lookup_symbol before applying non-trivial call/branch lemmas
downgrade this route and choose another current-view navigation item

moves.title: Proof-route context
moves.guidance: Inspect asymmetric instrumentation region: one-sided live state t1, ti must be budgeted.
moves.applicability: Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.

moves.title: Proof-route context
moves.guidance: Inspect sample coupling budget: t0{1}/t0{2} <$ dpoly_out; extra state t1, ti.
moves.applicability: Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.

moves.title: Program-shape context
moves.guidance: Inspect current procedure region summary: paired sample dpoly_out; asymmetric instrumentation right.
moves.applicability: Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.

moves.title: Proof-route context
moves.guidance: Structured procedure frontier: sample_frontier; primary: choose a rnd coupling/lossless family from sampled variables, distributions, and the post relation
moves.applicability: Use this as route-selection context; it is not a tactic to run as-is.

route_health.message: This looks like pRHL mid-surgery with a large postcondition. A common expert move is to use `conseq` to weaken to a smaller relational post, then use `sim` or automation.
evidence: `conseq` is useful after the frontier is already aligned enough to name a smaller relation
route_health.anti_route: Do not use `conseq` as a blind replacement for a missing call or loop invariant; if residuals need facts not carried by the last boundary, repair that boundary first.

structural_transitions.why_here: The current pRHL seq-cut exposes a large postcondition. Probe `wp.` if you want to test entry into the real post-wp suffix/surgery workbench.
structural_transitions.decision: Probe this tactic if you want to test entry into the post-wp surgery phase.
structural_transitions.after_commit: The next authoritative view will expose the real post-wp surgery workbench, so you do not need to mentally simulate now what will happen.
structural_transitions.if_wrong_after_commit: Use `undo_last_step` or `undo_to_checkpoint` if the committed route is wrong.

cut_position.tactic: seq 11 11 : (={Mem.lc, Mem.log, glob BNR, UFCMA.log, UFCMA.cbad1, UFCMA_l.lbad1, RO.m, SplitC2.I2.RO.m, SplitD.ROF.RO.m} /\ ={n, a, c1, p0, lt} /\ p{1} = p{2} /\ UFCMA_li.i{2} = nth0 /\ UFCMA_li.cbadi{2} = b2i (nth0 < size UFCMA_l.lbad1{2}) /\ UFCMA_li.badi{2} = (nth0 < size UFCMA_l.lbad1{2} /\ (nth (w1, w2) UFCMA_l.lbad1{2} nth0).`1 = (nth (w1, w2) UFCMA_l.lbad1{2} nth0).`2)).

frontier_alignment.summary: Frontier alignment: setup statements appear before the current frontier; sampling steps are visible inside the frontier. Use this as a proof-reader map before choosing while/rnd/seq/call tactics.

first_instruction_alignment.proof_read: The first visible statements have different heads; align or consume setup statements before using a symmetric branch/loop/call tactic.

rows.left: no left-side setup before this frontier
rows.right: ti <- nth witness lt (UFCMA_li.i - size UFCMA_l.lbad1)
rows.proof_read: The right side has setup before the shared frontier; relate it to the precondition or carry the initialized state in the next invariant/cut.
Both sides sample from the same distribution; once the state relation is right, the sampling step can usually be handled by a direct coupling or residual automation.

rows.left: no matching left-side sample at this frontier
rows.proof_read: This is a sampling frontier; choose a coupling or distribution argument that connects the sampled values.

procedure_navigation.how_to_use: Positions for choosing wp/sp depth, which `if` to rcond, and where the next loop/call frontier is. Facts only — pick the tactic and the branch direction yourself.

right.note: count from frontier alignment; the body parser under-counted the asymmetric (right-only) setup statements (0 parsed)

absorb_depth.sp_hint: sp 0 1 / wp -0 -1 absorbs the straight-line setup (0 left, 1 right) before the next frontier

checks: Read coverage and missing live facts before committing a seq cut.
A runnable seq candidate is not evidence that the invariant is strong enough.

evidence: distribution or lossless token remains visible
obligation_families.effect: The remaining pure goal may include invertibility or lossless side conditions from an earlier sampling alignment.
obligation_families.limitations: No distribution lemma is selected by this surface.

obligation_families.effect: The pure goal exposes tuple/record projections or constructor equalities that can connect program variables to map/list keys.
obligation_families.limitations: Projection equalities are reported only when visible in the current text.

obligation_families.effect: The current proof point is dominated by local logical obligations rather than program-frontier movement.
obligation_families.limitations: Quantifier names are not normalized across EasyCrypt pretty-printer variants.

translation_facts: Decorations identify the program memory from which a term is read.
Pretty-printer memory names and introduced proof-context names can differ.
ambient_memory_translation.effect: Local claims in the pure tail depend on the currently introduced memory binders as well as displayed term decorations.

pure_tail_surface.effect: Summarizes verifier-visible pure-goal structure after program frontier actions have been discharged or hidden by local proof steps.
limitations: This panel records syntactic and proof-state facts from the current view.
It does not choose a tactic or instantiate a lemma.

inspect_lookup_handles.effect: All handles here are read-only manager requests; the committed EasyCrypt proof state stays unchanged.
inspect_lookup_handles.manager_note: Some requests may ask EasyCrypt or name resolution; wait for the manager result before choosing the next proof intent.

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need parsed goal shape, resolved names, or KB pattern hints.
The current cut or frontier context may expose a call route.
ask_manager_for.returns: call-ready/oracle-equiv context for the current frontier, plus the BODY of the procedure(s) called here (functor aliases resolved) so you can see which oracles/state your call invariant must preserve

ask_manager_for.use_when: You are considering `call (_: Inv)` and already have a concrete invariant expression you want to test first.
ask_manager_for.returns: a read-only preview of the obligations caused by that candidate invariant, including pre/init, call-preservation, oracle-side, postcondition, missing-fact signals, and extra conjuncts that may create avoidable obligations

ask_manager_for.use_when: The visible cut may depend on LHS/RHS statement alignment or missing live facts.
ask_manager_for.returns: swap/alignment context for the visible pRHL frontier

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need the valid form for call, seq, while, rnd, or rewrite.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid tactic forms and common traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: The visible cut/frontier may need indexed `sp i j` before branch tactics.
Mid-proof pRHL suffix surgery may need indexed `wp`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid indexed `wp` forms and suffix-alignment traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: Statement order may need a small `swap` before `sp`, `wp`, or `sim`.
A guarded branch may need `rcondt` after a case split or invariant fact.
A guarded branch may need `rcondf` after a case split or invariant fact.
A suffix proof may need `conseq` to weaken to a smaller relation before `sim`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `conseq` forms and pRHL memory-annotation traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: One side may have an extra sample or need an explicit sample coupling.
A known statement-order mismatch may need an eager/lazy transformation.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `eager` forms and when to prefer smaller surgery
